reason: The text (Exodus 12) demonstrates God's profound concern for the Hebrew people through multiple indicators: (1) God directly commands Moses and Aaron regarding the Israelites' liberation; (2) God grants 'favor' (חֵן) to the people in the sight of the Egyptians, enabling their plundering of Egypt's wealth; (3) God performs a direct act of deliverance, explicitly stating 'on this very day the Lord brought out the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt'; (4) God establishes commemorative laws and ordinances specifically for the Israelite community; (5) The passage repeatedly refers to God's protective intervention ('a night of watching/vigil to the Lord' - שִׁמֻּרִים לַיהוָה) established for 'all the sons of Israel throughout their generations.' The entire passage centers on God's personal, protective action toward the Hebrew people, demonstrating they hold significant importance in God's purposes and care.
